name: Heather Knapp
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
A bit about my approach to therapy: In therapy, I utilize various theories, modalities and approaches depending on your specific concerns and needs. I have worked in many different areas over the last 26 years including grief work with children and adults, PTSD, depression and anxiety. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is aimed at changing unhelpful thinking and also learned behavior patterns. I have found the use of art as a great way to express what cannot be said in words. I cannot say enough about importance of self care planning. What you can expect from our first session: Change in our lives does not happen overnight and remembering that change is a process. It is important to be kind and gentle to yourself along the way. This is something I strongly encourage. Small goals that are achievable are very important that can lead to those larger goals. Therapy should not feel stressful but something you look forward to that gives you more than what you started with.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I believe everyone is unique, important and valuable. Being able to be heard, understood, cared for and respected is something that I want you to feel when you are in therapy with me. I am currently the clinical director for a non-profit that helps families and children in grief. Loss changes life and having the right support to navigate through this is important. I feel I can provide you with the right tools and support. I have experience in working with those who have experienced a loss by suicide. I have walked this road myself and know the immense challenges and pain associated with this type of loss. I am here. I consider myself an athlete and know the struggles that can come along in the area of sports and competition. Feelings of low self worth, anxiety and fear of failure or not being able to be successful are constant worries as an athlete. Being able to find yourself and to enjoy what you do so well at is something that I can work with you on.

name: Aaron Armelie (HE/HIM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I strive to learn about and incorporate each person’s values, life experience, inherent wisdom and personal strengths into my work with them. My approach is very relational in nature, meaning, in addition to bringing my mind and professional experience to the care I provide, I also bring my heart. Over the years, I have had the honor of working with people who hold diverse identities and cultural backgrounds, and who come from many different walks of life. I believe that all of us, regardless of how different we are from one another, belong in this world and are deserving of respect, empathy and kindness. I strive to provide services that are culturally humble, equitable and inclusive. I believe that it is important to attend the broader context of people’s lives, strengthening those things that can promote health and wellbeing, such as meaningful relationships, and addressing factors that can contribute to social stress, such as marginalization and oppression.

name: Curtis White (HE/HIM)
approaches:
What I Specialize In
I specialize in helping individuals with the tension that comes with trauma, identity, life transitions, depression, anxiety, borderline personality disorder, and grief/loss=. In our sessions, we will explore who you are; your world, beliefs, and values. Together, we will use this understanding to promote change and growth. I empower individuals to heal themselves and cope with the challenges of life. Evidence-based practices that I most commonly use are Dialectical Behavioral Therapy, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, Mindfulness-based therapies, Integrated Family Systems
More About the People I Treat
My approach to counseling is a collaborative experience that draws on the awareness of the Self. How our thoughts, feelings, and beliefs influence relationships with the Self, as well as how our environment affects these facets of everyday life. With self-compassion, gaining awareness of our inner-world can lead to a greater understanding of how our life choices affect our emotional and physical well-being and can be a guide to a more authentic and balanced life. Specialty areas Include: Anxiety Borderline Personality Disorder Challenges associated with disabilities Coping Skills Depression Ethical non-monogamy Life Transitions Men's Issues Mood disorders Obsessive Compulsive Disorder Personal Growth Relationship Conflicts Stress Self-Esteem Systemic Oppression Trauma

name: Melissa DeForest (Peterson) (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background
My experience in the mental health field started in 2012, where I began my work in higher levels of eating disorder treatment (residential, partial hospitalization, and intensive outpatient). Most recently, I served as Regional Director of Clinical Services for Monte Nido, where I oversaw all five treatment facilities in the state of Oregon. In this time, I gained extensive experience working with teens, adults, and families going through the eating disorder recovery journey, and am a Certified Eating Disorder Specialist-Consultant. My graduate training is in working with intimate partners and families, and I believe wholeheartedly that communication and connection is essential to the healing process. I am trained in Cognitive Processing Therapy, an evidence based approach to trauma. I am a registered supervisor for LPC and LMFT Associates in the state of Oregon. In addition to Oregon, I am licensed to see clients virtually in California and Washington.
My Approach to Helping
I believe healing happens through relationships. My approach integrates attachment theory and emotion-focused therapy, focusing on authentic communication, emotional expression, and systemic change. I view each person as unique, shaped by their context, and believe behavior makes sense when considering one's life experiences. I help clients explore how unique systems, past experiences, and identities influence their lives, and collaboratively set goals for growth. As a HAES-informed, anti-diet, fat-positive provider, I am committed to respecting all bodies and challenging harmful societal expectations. I prioritize building strong, trustworthy, and collaborative relationships with my clients in order to support a safe space for emotional exploration, conflict resolution, and trauma processing. I believe in the possibility of change and growth for each of my clients. I see therapy as an opportunity for connection and I bring empathy, transparency, and humor to the space.

name: Leslie Gellert (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I specialize in working with clients during and after pregnancy, kids and teens. In grad school, I spent a year interning in a women's clinic and have worked with perinatal clients throughout my 16 years as a therapist. I also spent over 4 years in school based primary care clinics as a behavioral health provider working with kids 5-18 years old. I use evidence based therapies and a client centered approach to empower individuals and families.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
Therapy is a collaborative journey where safety, trust and authenticity create the foundation for meaningful growth. My approach is grounded in empathy and deep respect for each person's unique story. I am interested in trauma informed care, mindfulness and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy. I believe that every person has the potential for growth, resilience and lasting transformation.

name: Morgan Fitz Gibbon (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
Licensed Marriage and Family Therapist - OR T1128 Founder - Rebel Heart Therapy Crisis Intervention Team Member - Burning Man Emergency Services Former Clinical Supervisor - Lewis & Clark Counseling Ctr Approved Supervisor - American Association of Marriage and Family Therapy Master of Arts in Clinical Psychology, LGBT Affirmative Psychology Bachelor of Arts in Psychology and Deaf Studies I absolutely love what I do to help my clients because I’ve been there. I’m queer, kinky, and have 20 years of experience in ethically non-monogamous relationships. My life has been a zig-zaggy line with a whole lot of challenges in my path. From figuring out myself to my relationships with others I’ve gone through it. Because I couldn’t find therapists who understood what I was going through, I put in a lot of work and research to get educated on how to help myself. I became a therapist because I didn’t want anyone else to experience the bad therapy I had. I want to share with you what I’

name: Meaghan Lewis (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My approach to psychotherapy is evidence-based, compassionate, and tailored to your needs. I consider therapy a collaborative process and would love to work with you to create meaningful and lasting changes. I earned my Ph.D. in clinical psychology at Western Michigan University. I also completed my pre-doctoral internship at the VA Pacific Islands Health Care System in Honolulu, HI and subsequently relocated to the Pacific Northwest to complete a three year VA advanced fellowship in mental illness research and treatment at the VA Portland Health Care System. As an intervention-oriented psychologist, my goal is to help you see results quickly and make improvements that last. I work together with my clients to provide the treatments that research says work and to help you function better now. If you are struggling, I may be able to help.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am committed to anti-racist practice, women's empowerment, and welcome clients from all backgrounds. I believe that Black Lives Matter and strive to be allied with the BIPOC community as well as the LGBTQIA+ communities. I also consider myself a body positive psychologist. During therapy, I welcome conversations related to diversity, equity, and inclusion including microaggressions that occur within and outside of the therapeutic context. I am particularly passionate about helping women and persons with marginalized identities to overcome systems of oppression that present in their daily lives and to live in a way that is fulfilling and in alignment with personal values.

name: Ivy Ross (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background
I have a Bachelors degree from Western Oregon University and aMasters degree from George Fox University in Marriage and Family Therapy. I have been practicing as a counselor for almost 20 years. I am also a Registered Play Therapist Supervisor and Clinical Supervisor. I hold a certification in Animal Assisted Human Health and Play Therapy.
More About the People I Treat
I see children, teens, and adults in my practice. Social justice and LGBTQ issues are important to me and I am a strong ally. I am also a fierce advocate for children and teens.

name: Lily Suhanovsky (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I take an integrative, holistic approach to therapy, drawing from psychodynamic, somatic, and relational-cultural perspectives. We explore not just your current struggles but also the deeper patterns and life experiences that have shaped you. My work is rooted in presence, compassion, and honoring your innate wisdom—believing healing comes from reconnecting with parts of yourself that may have been disowned or unacknowledged. In sessions, we focus on shifting distressing patterns, processing emotions, cultivating self-compassion, and engaging in grounding or movement-based practices to regulate your nervous system. I integrate neuroscience and somatic awareness to deepen your mind-body connection, build emotional resilience, and create lasting change. The therapeutic relationship is a powerful container for healing. Through warmth and collaboration, I support you in reconnecting with your intuition, empowering you to live authentically and create greater harmony in your life.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I hold a Master of Arts in Professional Mental Health Counseling from Lewis & Clark Graduate School of Education and Counseling, alongside a Bachelor of Science in Neuroscience from Union College. My studies included interpersonal neurobiology and Polyvagal Theory, which inform my work with the mind-body connection. I am also trained in Brainspotting through the Pacific Trauma Institute, which allows me to support clients in processing trauma. Additional certifications in Yin Yoga and Yoga for Trauma further enrich my holistic approach to healing.

name: Andrea Mize (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I offer both brief problem-oriented, solution-focused therapy (6-20 sessions) and longer-term, open-ended psychotherapy depending upon your needs. I provide support and compassion while challenging you to move towards your stated goals. Our sessions will be more of a conversation and collaboration with feedback flowing both directions to help you make the movement you seek. Productive therapy and life change happens when a solid, trusting relationship forms between client and therapist. One of my strengths as a therapist is forming that bond quickly with my clients so that we can identify and address problems together. While therapy takes time, I endeavor to help the change process along, providing encouragement and insight to aid carefully yet efficient growth. I encourage an open dialogue about how our fit is in sessions to ensure you are receiving the best support and aid that I can provide.
My Values as a Therapist
I share many identities with the people I serve and have a long personal history with invisibility. I bring a personal and professional understanding of the toll invisibility takes. While many people have identities reflected in community membership, we can often feel separated from these spaces and people. I believe most of us don't fit neatly into boxes, including ones designed for those of us left out of traditional spaces. Sometimes we don't feel seen in our relationships even if we do not experience invisibility or marginality in kthe world. I recognize that we can all feel missed and misunderstood no matter who and how we are. A sense of belonging and inclusion are some of the key factors in mental wellness. I strive to help my clients find safety, security and belonging in themselves to carry with them when encountering places, spaces and situations where they do not feel supported or understood.

name: Timothy (Tim) Holt (HE/HIM)
approaches:
My Background
I'm a psychiatric nurse practitioner, board certified, who has practiced in the mental health/psychiatric field for 27 years, which began after my education and additional training thru Oregon Health Sciences University in Portland, Oregon. I have treated the majority of psychiatric disorders that can be seen in such settings as outpatient mental health clinics in rural and urban settings, county jail and prison facilities, outpatient and inpatient substance abuse treatment facilities, adult foster homes for those with developmental disabilities/mental illness, psychiatric hospitals, and as a consultant to certain organizations at the state level that determine disability status. I use multiple tools in treating psychiatric disorders, but a frequently used tool is the use of medication and the use of cognitive behavioral therapy.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I believe we should treat each individual with dignity, respect, and kindness and the hope that this would be reciprocated by the other individual. However, I realize this may not be the case with each individual and it's in this instance to set appropriate boundaries, especially to reinforce the importance of self-worth. I believe connection with others is important and for this to be an opportunity for self-growth, as well as lifting up those who are around us.

name: Landon Michaels (HE/HIM/HIS)
approaches:
What I Specialize In
have specialty training in working with survivors of childhood abuse using a novel treatment called Contextual Trauma Therapy. This treatment aims to help recover not only from the trauma itself, but also the dysfunctional environment in which it occurs. My skillset is uniquely built to address the wide array of issues that can result from PTSD/C-PTSD as I believe you have to specialize in more than just trauma to help work towards resolution.

name: Katie Clark, LMHC, LPC (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
As a solution-focused, cognitive-behavioral therapist, my goal is to help you uncover your true potential and lead a life worth celebrating. While you can't change difficult situations of the past, we can work together to better understand and resolve the challenges in your life. By applying complementary therapy approaches and techniques, we will unearth long-standing behavior patterns or negative perceptions that may be holding you back from experiencing a more fulfilling, balanced, and meaningful life. I will work to build an individualized treatment plan in collaboration with you, so you can get the most from our time together.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am a Nationally Certified Counselor (NCC), Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC), and Licensed Mental Health Counselor (LMHC) registered with the states of Oregon and Washington. I conduct myself and my services in accordance with the ethics and guidelines set forth from the Washington and Oregon Boards of Licensed Professional Counselors and Therapists, as well as the ACA Ethical Code of the Oregon Counseling Association and the American Counseling Association. I am also a member of the ACA. I have a Master’s degree in Counseling Psychology from Pacific University and a Bachelor’s degree in Psychology with an emphasis in Business Administration from UCLA. In addition to my education and clinical training, I have significant and varied professional and life experiences to draw on in service of the needs of my clients.

name: Griffin Oakley (HE/HIM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My approach to therapy is compassionate, collaborative, and tailored to you. I believe that healing happens in a safe, nonjudgmental space where you feel truly seen and heard. Using an integrative approach, I draw from CBT, DBT, ACT, Gestalt, and Solution-Focused Therapy to create a plan that fits your unique needs. Whether you need help managing anxiety, processing trauma, exploring identity, or building self-esteem, I’ll meet you where you are with warmth and understanding. I focus on helping you uncover patterns, develop self-awareness, and build practical coping skills so you can navigate life’s challenges with more confidence and clarity. I work at your pace, offering both gentle guidance and empowering challenges to help you grow. My goal is to help you feel more connected to yourself, your emotions, and your values, so you can move forward with greater self-compassion and resilience. You don’t have to do this alone—I’m here to help.
My Values as a Therapist
As a therapist, my work is guided by values of compassion, affirmation, and empowerment. I create a safe, nonjudgmental space where you can explore your thoughts and feelings freely. I honor your unique lived experience, recognizing the impact of trauma, oppression, and identity on mental health. My approach is client-centered, using CBT, DBT, ACT, and Gestalt to help you find practical tools while building self-awareness. Therapy is not about fixing; it’s about empowering you to heal and grow at your own pace. I focus on helping you trust yourself, develop resilience, and move forward with hope and self-compassion. You deserve support, and I’m here to walk beside you, guiding you toward a fulfilling, authentic life.

name: Audra Lee
approaches:
How I Can Help You
Together we’ll uncover the truth of what your pain is trying to tell you about yourself, what’s really important to you, and what is and is not working in your life. This is such an important precursor to lasting change - but it’s not easy! My style is integrative and acknowledges the importance of body/mind/soul in treating underlying mental and emotional imbalances which disrupt a sense of well-being by interfering with the natural process of digesting and integrating life experiences. I can help you develop self-awareness, understanding and acceptance through collaborative, non-judgmental exploration of all aspects of life.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am a Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) in Oregon and Texas, a Licensed Mental Health Counselor (LMHC) in Washington, a Licensed Professional Clinical Counselor (LPCC) in New Mexico, and a Licensed Clinical Professional Counselor (LCPC) in Montana. I have two Master’s degrees, one in Molecular Biology and one in Professional Mental Health Counseling. I am also a certified Ayurvedic Practitioner I integrate Ayurvedic Psychology into my therapeutic approach. I am committed to a life-long process of learning, and strive to work from a place of cultural humility to increase my awareness, knowledge and skills both personally and professionally. I have a special interest in working with other healthcare professionals, including doctors and other psychotherapists, as well as college students and clients healing from complex trauma.

name: Alison Jones
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I believe we are all born with the innate capacity to be connected to our feelings and needs, without judgement or shame. Experiences in life, and sociocultural messages, all inform and influence our understanding of ourselves, our feelings of worthiness, and our connection with these deeper parts of ourselves. Sometimes, in experiences of trauma or prolonged stress, we develop ways of existing and coping that are necessary for our survival, but over time may no longer be needed, and can decrease feelings of connection, with self & others. You are not broken, you were born with innate wisdom, and therapy can support the process of reconnecting with the insight that is your birthright. Therapy can be supportive of quieting the noise, sinking into this deeper inner knowing, and support living a life rooted in authenticity, self-trust and self-compassion.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I have over a decade of experience working with individuals impacted by a broad spectrum of lived experiences, including trauma, depression and anxiety, who also experienced impact by systems and oppression. My theoretical orientation is most often more holistic and fluid to meet your goals and areas of interest or focus, and highly informed by utilizing an anti-oppression, pro-liberation lens, rooted in liberation psychology, intersectional feminist theory, systems theory and humanistic psychology. My clinical lens is trauma and racial justice informed, strengths based, and responsive to supporting you at all stages of life. I also have interest in all mindfulness-based approaches, as well as Eastern philosophy, and my theoretical orientation and perspective is also influenced by the brilliant work of Pema Chodron, bell hooks and Angela Davis.

name: Andrew Rose (HE/HIM/HIS)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I practice depth psychology, meaning I believe that healing can only come about when we begin to understand the root causes of symptoms. We are all living in a world defined by the people around us, yet also by our absolute individuality and freedom of choice. Together with my patients, we work to understand how one’s view of reality colors other parts one’s life, including affect and mood. Rather than try to talk patients out of these feelings, I prefer to work on understanding how one’s place in existence can become such that one’s days become darker and the will to live in the world and the search for joy seem meaningless. To me, meaning is essential for any successful therapy. We have to find, through our choices and our view of reality, a way toward meaning. Meaning cannot be bestowed on you, and it must be reinvigorated regularly, but it is the most satisfying part of being alive.
My Values as a Therapist
The first principle I rely on as a therapist is that of autonomy. Ultimately, it is the patient’s decision how much they want to share in therapy, or if they even wish to continue seeing a clinician. I also strongly value ethics. Most therapists take their ethical responsibilities seriously, but, regrettably not all. Confidentiality is more than the black-and-white text of HIPAA; it is a sacred trust. Patients must be able to trust that their clinician will withhold personal opinions and always offer unconditional positive regard. As therapists, we cannot make decisions for you, but helping you understand the impact of a decision and what is really at stake is a shared goal.

name: Eshe Boyette (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I emphasize a relational approach in therapy, as I believe our therapeutic relationship is key to our work together. I will encourage you to be your most authentic self and live in line with your values using a blend of therapies. My primary approaches include Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, Parts Work, and Acceptance & Commitment Therapy. I’m here to support you at any stage of your recovery journey. Starting therapy with someone new can feel daunting, but my goal is to create a relaxed and comfortable environment. Don’t hesitate to reach out with questions. I look forward to meeting you!
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
As a therapist who understands trauma recovery on a personal level, I am passionate about walking alongside others on their path to healing. It's an honor to support people in their recovery process, particularly because I personally know the strength that it takes. It is incredibly rewarding to watch people begin to believe in themselves again. In my free time, I like to practice yoga, go to the gym, read, cook, and spend time outside hiking or just hanging out.

name: Zoë Muller (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
Zoë incorporates concepts and individual modalities associated with life spandevelopment, behavioral change through CBT and ACT, play therapy, Adlerian child guidance, mindfulness and teaching self-regulation and social skills to neurodivergent population. She works collaboratively with families, caregivers, educators and other important players in the child’s environment to ensure a wrap-around support system that fosters growth and development. Zoe is fluent in Russian.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
Zoë believes that parenting is one of the most important roles a person can undertake in their life, and that navigating the unique challenges of this role can be a gateway to a full and meaningful existence. She has extensive experience in providing behavioral support to parents of children with anxiety, OCD and neurodivergence, as well as evidence-based solution-oriented support to families dealing with mental health challenges.

name: Morgan Lyons (SHE/HER OR THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Working from a stance of curiosity, compassion, and non-judgment, my hope is to partner with you in building the skills and tools to navigate whatever life throws at you. Through our work together, I will aim to support you in fostering the vulnerability to love yourself and others, the strength to approach pain, and the flexibility to touch meaning and passion even on your hardest days. My practice is grounded in Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, Dialectical Behavioral Therapy, and other mindfulness-based approaches. With over a decade of experience providing counseling services, much of my work has focused on supporting the LGBTQIA+ community, survivors of violence and abuse, unhoused youth, and individuals struggling with self-harm and suicidality.
My Values as a Therapist
My most cherished values include community, compassion, self-determination, and joy and I strive to embody these as a provider. I am committed to engaging in anti-oppressive practices and actively work to uphold anti-racist, trauma-informed, sex-positive, and body-positive lens in my practice. The principles of Harm Reduction are important to me and central to the way I approach my work. If it sounds like I might be a good fit for you, give me a call or a text! We can schedule a consultation and talk through what you’re looking for. Alternatively, if you would like to know more about me, my background, or training before reaching out, feel free to check out my website linked on this page.

name: Danesh Oleshko LMFT (HE / HIM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My commitment is to provide effective therapy. You can expect genuine connection, active listening and engaged style with me. I'm curious and open-hearted. I studied Integral Counseling Psychology at the nation's leading school in this field: California Institute of Integral Science (CIIS) in San Francisco. I've been blessed with exceptional teachers and guides, leaders in the field of psychodynamic, person-centered and existential psychotherapy. Parallel to formal education, I've also been apprenticing with indigenous tradition keepers of ceremonial use of Sacred Plant medicine Ayahuasca: consciousness - expanding and purifying experience. I have specialized in mental health and addiction therapy by co-founding and developing holistic treatment programs for recovery, as well as working in an community mental health agency in Ashland that facilitated recovery alongside with family therapy, couples therapy and trauma-informed care. Welcome to my healing practice. 
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
My core values are professional competence, empathy and genuine presence. I strive to learn and approach each session with an open heart. Becoming a therapist is an outcome of a long search my true calling in life. I feel blessed to have my purpose. As part of my learning, I've studied with leaders in the field of depth psychology; I've apprenticed under guidance of indigenous tradition keepers. I discovered deep passion for treatment of addiction and trauma: have pioneered and developed holistic treatment programs in South America. Currently, my private practice integrates psychotherapy with Ayurveda and traditional healing ceremonies with sacred plants of the Amazonian region. I live simple life on the Oregon coast with my wife and two curious dogs. I do yoga everyday, run in the woods and take my wooden boat on adventures in the Bay. In addition to providing depth therapy, I love to play music, Banya - finish sauna & hugging people I love.

name: Shawna Oliver, LCSW (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I believe in creating an emotionally safe environment where individuals and couples can process feelings, develop new insights, overcome negative thinking, improve communications and set healthier boundaries. I believe counseling is a collaborative process and work with individuals and couple in developing goals that will best fit their needs. Not one mode of therapy works for all and I often use an eclectic approach designed to address the issues and achieve the desired goals.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am a Licensed Clinical Social Worker and have been working in the human services field for the last 25 years. During my career I have had the opportunity to work with individuals and families who have experienced a variety of life crisis’ such as; depression, anxiety , divorce, abuse, relationship and marriage issues, parenting issues and grief and loss.

name: Jessica Barnese (SHE/HER)
approaches:
What I Specialize In
My work mainly draws on the Hakomi Method, in which clients are guided to slow down and tune into their present-moment experience so we can move beyond just the story and truly work with what arises as they relive it. We explore the deeply held belief structures that shape how current challenges are felt and understood and update outdated patterns to create newer, more aligned perspectives. I work with many clients who need support moving through life transitions, developmental trauma, and relational trauma. I deeply value working with female bodied people in the perimenopausal to postmenopausal stages of life, helping them uncover their innate wisdom during this powerful time. I also enjoy working with fellow therapists curious about somatic approaches, as they often find this work deeply enriching for their personal growth and professional practice.
My Approach to Helping
In our work together, it is my goal to help you access the parts of yourself that are striving to come forward so that you can live a more satisfying and fulfilling life. I do not work to define what is right for you, but rather observe and collaborate with you to find the direction your own individual life force is moving you. My approach is based in the integration of mindfulness and somatic awareness to help heal past wounds, foster emotional and mental clarity, and cultivate deeper relationships. I use mindfulness to uncover old patterns and core beliefs, and use the therapeutic relationship to address relational and attachment issues in real time. The goal of our work together is to help you develop a secure sense of self and feel access to clear choice in your life, as well as the ability to form healthy relationships in ways that fit for you.

name: Ann DeWitt
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I bring wit and wisdom into our work. I share many practical tools and resources, and offer empathy and support to put them to use in your life. I have been teaching parenting classes for 25 years and have helped hundreds of couples increase affinity and communication, clarify their values, and get on the same parenting page. I find a sense of humor is essential in getting through this thing called life. We will laugh together and hopefully bring some lightness back into your days.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I earned by Master's Degree in Clinical Psychology in 1995. My emphasis has always been on helping people create fulfilling and healthy family relationships. I am licensed in California and Oregon.

name: Larkin Corrigan (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I offer a safe, nonjudgmental space where you can process your emotions and be heard and understood. Through this process, you will have the opportunity to gain coping skills to help you better manage stressful situations and relationships. We will build a collaborative partnership in which we will work together to create strategies that will empower you to achieve your goals. I have experience supporting clients who are adjusting to major life transitions, coping with serious illness, and dealing with anxiety, depression, grief and loss, family conflict, and caregiver challenges. As a clinical social worker, I am trained to consider how systems of oppression impact your lived experience and am aware of how such systems can often add to the challenges you’re facing.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am excited to join you on your journey of growth and healing. Together, we will build a collaborative partnership in which we will create strategies to empower you to achieve your goals. Take the first step by reaching out to me to schedule a complimentary 15 minute session to see if I am a good fit for your therapy goals.

name: Cassie Cole (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I hope to create a strong, collaborative, and egalitarian therapeutic relationship with my clients ensuring they feel heard, and respected throughout the process. I foster awareness and growth in the present moment, helping people explore how their life experiences have shaped their emotional worlds while striving towards forward movement with clarity and confidence. To create lasting transformation, I emphasize the interconnectedness of emotional, physical, and spiritual health. By showing up authentically with warmth and curiosity I work to honor a safe space for exploration, using humor and empathy to guide clients toward their goals.
Why I am a Good Fit for You
I am interested in helping people navigate their personal and relational challenges, always meeting them where they are at with constant humility and a willingness to remain teachable. I know by my own experience finding a therapist is confusing , frustrating and more difficult than it needs to be. I can’t possibly put all you need to know about me as a therapist (I sure wish I could) in these little boxes. I encourage you to try a few sessions out and decide how you feel after that. I will always support what’s best for you. And if you made it this far in the profile I look forward to working with you!

name: Shelby Tuthill (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My PhD background gives me a strong background in research-supported forms of treatment, which I integrate with collaborative, client-centered care that is tailored to your specific needs. I use an integrative approach, drawing from evidence-based orientations such as Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), and Interpersonal Process Therapy. Underlying all my work is the belief that individuals are best understood in context: the relationships, institutions, and broader sociopolitical systems in which they exist. Thus, my approach also centers a framework that openly and explicitly acknowledges power, oppression, and privilege. All of these factors are woven together in therapy to create a treatment plan and approach that works for you - your specific goals, needs, preferences, identities, and material realities.
More About the People I Treat
I am especially interested in working with members of the LGBTQ+ community, a community with which I identify. I have been out for almost ten years and strongly value supporting other community members in their journey. The focus of my doctoral dissertation, an area of passion for me, was on contributors and barriers to connection and sense of community among LGBTQ+ young adults. Another aspect of my work outside of individual and couples therapy is psychodiagnostic assessment. This is a process by which clients seek clarity about their mental health concerns. These evaluations may focus more on clarifying the nature of mood-related or interpersonal concerns, or may involve more specific testing for diagnoses such as ADHD. I offer in-person services on Wednesdays in Lake Oswego or telehealth services on other days of the week.

name: Danielle Meyer (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I specialize in the treatment of women's issues, eating disorders, mood disorders, trauma, grief and loss, and life transitions. I aim to help individuals manage difficult emotions and recover a lost sense of self and meaning. We do this through the Jungian lens of approaching and integrating the unconscious through dreams, symbols, the imagination, and archetypes. By using internal family systems I help clients form new attitudes towards their inner parts, and cultivate a center of self. A lot of this work is based around self-compassion and attachment theory. By using re-parenting tools with ourselves and our parts, we can feel more whole and less conflicted by difficult emotions in the body. I also work with trauma and stress and find art therapy, internal family systems, and a mixture of dreamwork or imaginative work can be helpful for creating meaning and resilience.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
As a Jungian psychotherapist I believe that there is a collective unconscious that we are all connected to. I believe in a holistic appraoch to understanding symptoms as messengers from the unconscious that offer helpful information. I believe all of our inner parts possess an innate wisdom that is unique to each person and that by connecting to and healing these split off parts, one can connect to a deep sense of personal meaning. This meaning can also be found through art-making, dreamwork, imaginative/visualization exercises, and journaling prompts. I also believe that archetypal astrology can be a helpful tool in psychotherapy, which can help connect daily life experiences to deeper underlying patterns that inform our lives. I also believe in challenging systems of oppression, and as a specialist in the field of eating disorders I support anti-diet and health at every size approaches to healing.

name: Carly Rubin (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
Hi! I'm Carly (she/her). I’m a Licensed Marriage & Family Therapist, adjunct professor, and supervisor to therapists-in-training. Working with intimate partners and helping people navigate relationship dynamics is at the heart of my life’s work. I believe in individual and collective healing and liberation for all people through trauma-informed relational work that honors everyone’s unique experience. I use methods like Emotionally Focused Therapy (EFT), Internal Family System (IFS), attachment theory, and somatic practices that encourage self-compassion and open us to transformational connection.
More About the People I Treat
I am honored to work with people of all gender expressions, sexual orientations, neurotypes, relationship models, races, religions, ages, sizes, and disabilities. I am a sex-positive therapist and work from a trauma-informed perspective. Let me support you in discovering the tools you need to help you thrive!

name: Sakura Counseling (SHE/HE/THEY)
approaches:
Our Approach to Helping
We believe in creating collaborative healing relationships where your therapist will work with you to figure out the changes you want to make, then create an attainable, realistic plan of healing. We will then prepare you for the changes to come and develop tools needed to successfully integrate those changes into your current environment. We believe that you are the expert of your experiences and needs, so throughout our collaboration, we aim to allow you to feel free of guilt and shame and safe to be wholly, authentically you as you gain strength of understanding, while building new methods to cope and overcome.
Our Personal Beliefs and Interests
As a group practice, we have the ability to provide therapy for most issues with specialized areas of Trauma, Social Justice, Mutli-cultural concerns, Anxiety, Suicide, Grief and Loss, Domestic Violence, Couples Relationship Repair...just to name a few. We work with all ages and all walks of life without judgement and thoroughly enjoy working with individuals, couples and families. To find out more about our team go to sakuracounseling.org. We currently have one counselor, Nancy, who works with Moda. All other counselors are OHP, Cigna, Kaiser, Reegence and OOP only.

name: Tara Rose (SHE/THEY)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I practice from a trauma informed, humanistic, anti-oppressive, and strengths-based framework. I also draw from parts/systems work, relational, and narratives therapies and EMDR. I am committed to providing gender and identity affirming care that allows for safe exploration to become closer to your true self. I also have a strong focus in trauma recovery and how the brain and nervous system have learned to adapt to keep you safe. We will connect about how your experiences have shaped thoughts about yourself, behaviors, and emotions, and how these create barriers for the goals you have in your life. We will also explore and consider the impacts of systemic oppression on your identity and sense of self. I take a non-pathologizing approach to working my clients, with a particular focus on those with marginalized identities. I wholeheartedly believe in your ability to be more connected with yourself and am excited to be with you on your journey.

name: Annalise Ophelian (THEY/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My work is geared toward supporting you in tapping into your existing strengths (I promise you have them!), identifying and strategizing around external issues that we often have no control over, and honing in on where you can affect change and call in the support you need and deserve. My approach is collaborative, supportive, solution-focused, and grounded in a social justice framework that acknowledges the role of institutional and systemic marginalization in our available resources, paths for healing, and sense of well-being. I believe diagnostic categories are only as useful as they provide a shorthand for you to describe yourself and your experiences. I hold them lightly, and always follow the lead of those I'm working with when it comes to the way you understand yourself. You are the expert in your own experience; I'm here to help you organize and make the most of this expertise, be a supportive guide, and help you mobilize the tools and resources you need to thrive.
My Values as a Therapist
I provide culturally responsive trauma-informed cognitive behavioral therapy to help you feel resilient, resourced, and supported in unstable times. My work is grounded in a social justice framework that acknowledges the role of institutional and systemic marginalization in our available resources, paths for healing, and sense of well-being. I'm also a neuroscience geek who is always looking at the mind-body connection when it comes to how we survive and thrive. I'm a big believer in the power of popular culture (film, TV, comics, games, and books) to offer allegories and language for shared experience. My practice is informed by disability justice and anti-colonial principles, and I'm a prison abolitionist who has provided healthcare within the Prison Industrial Complex and understands firsthand that there's no "justice" in criminal justice systems. I offer pro bono support letters for gender affirming medical procedures.

name: Kyan Osher
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
My foundation to counseling is a Humanistic or “person-centered” approach. I believe that all humans have an innate wisdom and that it’s my role to help my clients discover their own path. I have great respect for each person’s individual process and employ multiple techniques to facilitate that discovery, including mindfulness, cognitive behavioral therapy, family systems therapy, internal family systems therapy and interpersonal therapy.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I hold a Masters of Arts (MA) degree in Counseling from Pacific University in Oregon. I received a Bachelor's degree in Nutrition and Pre-Medicine from California Polytechnic University. In addition to my eduction and clinical training, I have some varied life experiences to help me counsel my clients.

name: Jocelyn Wagman (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
As a therapist, I draw from practices based in mindfulness, self-compassion, polyvagal theory, internal family systems, and trauma-informed care. I lean into my high-sensitivity to offer warm, connected, compassionate therapy, and can help other highly-sensitive people learn about and work with this trait. I completed my graduate training in social work in 2015 and also hold a Masters degree in public health. I’ve worked extensively with people navigating serious illness and loss and their families in hospital settings. I regularly participate in trainings and consultation groups to continue to grow as a therapist. I am a parent and a step-parent and am continually learning and stretching from these experiences too.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I chose social work because of its emphasis on social justice. I believe that most of the struggles we experience are rooted in various forms of oppression, and actively participating in dismantling these oppressive structures is important to me. I strive for anti-racism and all forms of anti-oppressive practice. I especially enjoy working with parents because of how rich my own experience step-parenting and parenting is. There are constant invitations to confront my own old wounding and grow as a parent. Over the years of my training, I've learned so much from the field of interpersonal neurobiology and from child development perspectives like RIE that have helped me as a parent - helping other parents learn how to understand their own and their children's behavior is immensely rewarding.

name: Timothy Anderson
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I work collaboratively with folks by exploring thoughts, feelings, and actions. We will do this as a means to gain insight and achieve wellness. We will also discuss tools and coping strategies to manage stressors and concerns. I am a licensed Clinical Psychologist. I hold a Doctorate degree in Clinical Psychology and Master’s degree in Counseling Psychology. I have practiced in the San Francisco, Central Pennsylvania, and Nebraska. I have been in Portland for the last 5 years.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I am committed to working to give the highest level of care to my patients. I value education and learning. I work continuously expand my education and skill set to meet the ever-changing needs of our community. I work to promote equality, diversity, and inclusion. I value the relational dynamics of therapy and work to provide a relationship most conducive to counseling. In my spare time I enjoy family, friends, community, reading graphic novels, hanging out with my golden retriever, skateboarding, and playing heavy metal music

name: Alyssa Moore (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I believe that we are all searching for ways to lead fulfilling lives, but sometimes we struggle to find the things that will help sustain us or the obstacles of life can begin to feel overwhelming. As a therapist, I help to identify the things that nourish each individual and act as a partner in learning strategies for moving through the difficult times. I believe that counseling is most effective when the client and the counselor collaborate on identifying individual strengths and opportunities for growth. This collaboration allows for the creation of a more vibrant life experience for the client. I believe that it is important to use treatments and techniques that have been carefully researched and found effective, so I take an evidence-based approach to therapy. I utilize an eclectic approach that draws heavily on cognitive behavioral therapy.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I hold a masters degree in Counseling Psychology from Pacific University, where I gained a strong foundation in assessment and diagnosis, individual counseling, group dynamics, and human development. I have a bachelor's degree in Psychology from Arizona State University, where I focused on human sexuality and learning and motivation. I have received specialized training in working with the LGBTQ community, and have significant experience working with LGBTQ identity issues, gender affirmation, and gender transition. My clinical experience has included group and individual work with children, youth, and adults in residential, outpatient,community based, and mandated settings.

name: Jess Brunelle (SHE/THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Are you not feeling seen or heard? Have you been coping the best that you can with depression, anxiety or trauma symptoms, but life is still a struggle? Let me help you on your journey to recovery. There are so many ways to help unique, individual people with their challenges. Each person has different needs and I treat each client with respect, dignity and autonomy. Collaboratively, we can create therapy goals and demystify the process together. I am a client-centered, licensed mental health therapist. I specialize in trauma therapy and working with youth. I have 15 +years experience using various modalities including trauma-informed CBT, DBT, Somatic Experiencing, psychoeducation, and trauma-informed Play and Expressive Arts Therapy. Also, I work with anxiety disorders, OCD and depression. Please take a chance and reach out. I am here to help.
Social Justice Issues I Care About
I am involved in the work of helping others. I am dedicated to continuing my own learning and healing journey and I feel that as a community, we are here to support each other in solidarity. I have worked for the LGBTQ+ community, of which I am a part of, for 30 years in various volunteer capacities. I am a non-binary, queer human with parents who are first-generation American. I am passionate about social justice for BIPOC, and the intersectionality between socioeconomic issues, multi-generational trauma, equal rights for all women, and lack of resources for groups of under represented individuals. I have researched and participated in programs aimed at expanding my knowledge about trauma and how it affects us cognitively, emotionally and somatically. Most importantly, learning how to support others as they seek to heal from and thrive with a trauma history. I specialize in neurodivergent challenges, as well as gaining insight around masking and honoring our unique view of the world.

name: Grace Carter (SHE/HER)
approaches:
How I Can Help You
I offer sessions that can target the specific areas that you are wanting to address. I offer trauma informed talk therapy and creative/somatic therapies for creative thinkers. Clients who work with me appreciate my warmth and compassion, and my illuminating insights. Check out my website and book a free consultation today!
Why I Became a Therapist
I’m no different from the clients I see. Like everyone else, I'm a human who has made many human mistakes. I've been through wonderful and difficult experiences. These experiences have shaped who I am, and how I help clients. Throughout my younger years as an adult, I struggled with emotional wounds that created real difficulty in my personal and professional life. During that time, I worked with a therapist whose warmth and guidance created a path for healing and peace. It was one of the most important and profound experiences of my adult life. I realized that I wanted to be a part of this journey with others, as a guide. I feel true awe and gratitude at being entrusted to do this work. I believe in this process and I have been through it myself. There’s no doubt, my life experiences are what led me to become the therapist I am today.

name: Emily Ott, LCSW (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I provide evidence-based treatments for adults who are interested in addressing symptoms of PTSD, OCD, anxiety and depression. I am trained in Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), and well as Exposure Response and Prevention (ERP). The type of treatment modalities provided will be unique and tailored to each individual depending on symptoms and most appropriate approach. I also offer trauma-informed Yin style yoga classes to support healing and nervous system regulation in the body. I am a certified yoga instructor (200 RYT) and enjoy implementing gentle movement and breathing techniques to invoke the “rest and digest” parasympathetic nervous system that puts the body at ease. This can radically counter trauma-related symptoms and chronic stress, which so many of us experience throughout life.
Why I Became a Therapist
Prior to moving to the PNW and becoming a mental health therapist, I was working in the New York City performing arts industry. While I still love the performing arts, the 2020 pandemic allowed me to reflect on my own values and goals, while also providing the time to go back to school for my Masters in Social Work from the University of Southern California. Similar to the performing arts, clinical social work includes the themes of valuing stories, cultivating empathy, and seeking to understand the human experience. This is a wonderful professional fit for me and I'm honored to have the opportunity to be a mental health provider.

name: Nadine Luensman (THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I am passionate about growing self-compassion and acceptance in my clients. I have years of experienced working with people using Health and Every Size and trauma informed care. My goal is to guide my clients into paradigm shifts that lead to healing and a more fulfilled life.
My Values as a Therapist
My number one values as a therapist are empowerment and autonomy. These values of guide me to work with my client in a way that emboldens their personal intuition to find healing that feels best to them. This usually includes work on self-discovery, self compassion, emotional regulation, and support through small and large life changes. I am especially passionate about walking alongside people from the LGBTQI+ community, promoting body size equality, and helping people who suffer from suicidal thoughts.

name: Kelsey Papadatos (SHE/HER)
approaches:
How I Can Help You
I am a Licensed Clinical Social Worker and have a Masters in Social Work, as well as a bachelors in both psychology and sociology. Since starting as a therapist, I have continued my learning and was trained in EDMR in 2023. I work from a trauma-informed, strengths-based, and culturally-affirming lens to consider how the systems clients live within affect how they interact with the world. I am passionate about working with folks living with the long and short term affects of complex trauma, LGBTQIA+ population, and young adults navigating difficult life transitions.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
No matter where you are in your healing journey, finding a new therapist can be overwhelming, so my goal is help cultivate a safe environment to best meet your goals and needs. I like to show up in the therapy space with warmth, humor when appropriate, transparency, and authenticity in effort to create a genuine and safe therapeutic relationship.

name: Helen Zwiebel (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Therapy is about the relationship and therapeutic alliance. We will create a safe space where you feel seen and heard. This space will be free from judgment and built on authenticity. We will work in collaboration with a strong focus on building confidence and trust in yourself. You will be provided guidance and support to process what life has presented you. Any goals set will be based on your values. Evidence-based practices used include Motivational Interviewing, Feedback Informed Treatment, Dialectical Behavioral Therapy, and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy.
Why I am a Good Fit for You
I am invested in helping this community. It is a privilege to help people define and feel empowered about their sexuality. I hope to be a part of the normalization of our bodies, desires, and behaviors. Specialties Consensual/Ethical Non-Monogamy (CNM/ENM) Relationship Issues Sexual Expression Stress and Life Transitions Also experienced in: Age group 18+, BDSM/kink, Body positivity, Coping with life changes, Enthusiastic consent, LGBTQIA+, Mindfulness, Pain management, Personal growth, Self-esteem, Severe and Persistent Mental Illness (SPMI), Sexual problems, Sleeping difficulties

name: Kristen Brown
approaches:
My Background
Kristen holds an Associate Degree in Nursing from Lane Community College in Eugene, Oregon, a Bachelor of Science in Nursing from Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU) in Portland, and a Master of Science in Nursing from Southern Adventist University. Starting her career as a Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA) in 2006, Kristen spent seven years in that role before advancing to Registered Nurse (RN) in 2013. She furthered her expertise by obtaining her PMHNP in 2022 and is currently pursuing her certification in integrative psychiatry, enhancing her ability to provide holistic mental health care.
Why I am a Good Fit for You
Kristen is particularly passionate about holistic health and Ayurveda, having studied the ancient practice rigorously. As a certified Ayurvedic Health Coach, she incorporates these principles into her patient care, promoting overall wellness alongside traditional psychiatric treatment. Her dedication to leadership and excellence was recognized with the Dean’s Leadership Award during her studies. Outside of her professional life, Kristen enjoys outdoor activities such as hiking, backpacking, snowshoeing, paddleboarding, and sailing. She is also passionate about yoga, meditation, and spiritual study, believing in the profound connection between mental well-being and a balanced lifestyle. Kristen is excited to join the Head Well clinic, where she looks forward to providing compassionate and comprehensive mental health care to her clients.

name: Rhys Pasimio (HE/HIM)
approaches:
How We Can Help You
Our philosophy of healing is informed by modalities such as Internal Family Systems, The Task Model utilized among CSAT practitioners, Somatic Experiencing, Narrative Therapy, Existential Therapy, select tenets from 12 Step Philosophy, current day neuroscience, and other evidenced based practices. We are further informed by ideas derived from practitioners including Dan Siegel, Gabor Mate, Richard Schwartz, Patrick Carnes, Jenna Riemersma, Philip Mamalakis, and various spiritual practices. NPC places a high value on supporting counseling students, and registered associates working towards licensure. Supervision is provided, being informed by IFS, Narrative, Existential, and process oriented approaches. In individual and group settings, we help you train the muscle that is the focus of your attention. In this, we help you grow in awareness of all your inward parts, behavioral and emotional cycles, and learn to have healing conversations within yourself, and with others.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I hold a Master of Arts in Counseling from Multnomah University. Major coursework included Integration of Psychology and Spirituality with emphases on Addictions Recovery and Social Concern. I completed additional coursework through Daystar Academy to earn my credential as Certified Alcohol and Drug Counselor II. My graduate internship was at the Salvation Army Adult Rehabilitation Center, providing individual and group counseling. My post-graduate practice was at Cascadia Behavioral Healthcare providing dual diagnosis counseling. As a licensed professional, I practiced with Western Psychological & Counseling Services and taught in the Master of Arts Counseling Program at Multnomah University, prior to starting New Pattern Counseling.

name: MindTree Holistic Counseling & Wellness Center
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
We have an amazing team of licensed counselors and wellness professionals who offer a multi-disciplinary approach that is customized to your needs. We work with clients of all ages who are experiencing depression, anxiety, trauma, grief and behavioral issues. We work with younger kids ( 3+), teens, adults, families, and couples You can find out more about us on our website. We currently offer both in person and Telehealth appointments. Our therapists are trained in one or more of the following: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, DBT, Internal Family Systems, Interpersonal Neurobiology, Play Therapy, Art therapy, CPRT ( Child parent relationship training), Attachment Therapy, Parenting Education, Couples & Sex Therapy, EMDR, Mindfulness based stress reduction, yoga, Energy Healing, Hypnotherapy, and Motivational Interviewing.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
Our guiding philosophy is that the mind and body do not operate separately, but rather interdependently. MindTree treats the person as a whole, by integrating the healing of mind and Body. You can gain greater physical health and mental well being when the mind and body are connected. At MindTree we have a team of independent providers who create holistic treatments that address your physical, emotional, social and spiritual needs. A multi-disciplinary approach is implemented, which incorporates treatments including, but not limited to, counseling, yoga, Reiki energy healing, Ayurveda, lifestyle alterations. The uniqueness of every individual deserves a thoughtful and customized treatment plan. Our diverse staff provides unique perspectives to the cultural, racial, spiritual, and social needs of our patients. Our providers respect diversity and work with clients from different racial/ ethnic backgrounds, different cultures and sexual orientations.

name: Arielle Ross (THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I incorporate mindfulness, creative interventions and personal writing into my practice, and draw heavily from emotion focused, attachment-based and internal family systems theories, which means I focus on learning to identify and name feelings, improve attachments to those close to us, create new meanings after we’ve experienced significant life changes, and offer our past and current selves empathy. I'm trained in EMDR and in Narrative Exposure Therapy, as well as DBT. I am committed to anti-racist and anti-colonial work, and draw from a feminist, queer-affirming & trauma-informed lens.
My Values as a Therapist
I believe we're all doing the best we can with the skills, knowledge and resources we have. I believe that we have an innate internal bent toward healing: that is, if we can heal, we will, and therapy can sometimes help us get out of our own way. I operate from a anti-oppressive, feminist, LGBTQ+ affirming and anti-racist lens, and am a really big history dork, so I like learning about your family history and how that might have impacted how you were raised, your core beliefs and how you were resourced or hurt by that history. In my free time I make food for mutual aid free fridges, am an active member of the Jewish community in Portland, and volunteer with climate advocacy organizations to get out the vote and advocate for divestment from fossil fuels. I also love dancing, hanging out with my partners, and playing board games.

name: Clifford Henry (THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I approach therapy with a gentle touch but don’t shy away from being straightforward when needed. I offer empathy but will also gently challenge you to grow. Humor is always welcome in our sessions—it’s great for breaking the ice, connecting us, & sometimes giving us a new way to look at things. My therapeutic approach is rooted in honoring your lived experience and innate wisdom that comes from your lived experiences. I believe you are the expert of your lived experience & meeting you where you are. I bring tools from Liberation Psychology, Emotion Focused Therapy, Gestalt, Internal Family Systems, Somatic Embodiment and BrainSpotting. At the core of my approach is rooted in the therapeutic relationship and collaboration. From our very first session, my goal is to foster hope and nurture trust. We will set clear goals to guide us, ensuring we work together towards genuine transformation, personal growth, and self-acceptance. I work with individuals, adults and couples.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I’m passionate about creating inclusive, affirming spaces where all clients feel safe and valued. As a BIPOC and Latinx counselor, a proud ally of the LGBTQ+ community, and an advocate for social justice, I believe everyone deserves access to compassionate mental health care. My journey toward becoming a counselor was fueled by a desire to help others uncover resilience, heal from past wounds, and experience meaningful transformation. I draw on trauma-informed, holistic approaches, with specialized training in relational therapy, mindfulness, and somatic techniques. When I’m not in the therapy room, I volunteer with the Liberation Institute a community-based mental health clinic as the Portland Director of Equitable & Inclusive Therapy Practices. My work and my values are intertwined: I believe in showing up for my clients with warmth, integrity, and a deep commitment to fostering growth and healing.

name: Kyong Yi (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Since 2003, I have dedicated my career to advancing mental health through both clinical practice and innovative research. With a Master’s in Social Work (2005) and extensive training in Emotion-Focused, Acceptance and Commitment, Compassion-Focused, Dialectical Behavioral, Cognitive Behavioral therapies, and Motivational Interviewing, my approach is rooted in rigorous science and genuine care. I work with individuals and groups to address trauma, anxiety, and relational challenges, empowering you to rediscover strength through vulnerability and connection. As a research therapist engaged in clinical trials for MDMA-assisted therapy for Social Anxiety Disorder, I bring pioneering insights and a deep commitment to helping you build resilience and meaningful relationships.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I believe that authentic healing emerges when we embrace vulnerability and cultivate genuine connection. Grounded in scientific evidence and heartfelt empathy, I see each person as capable of transformative growth. My passion for advancing psychedelic research fuels my commitment to explore innovative, integrative therapies that honor both mind and spirit. Outside the therapy room, I enjoy staying current with clinical innovations, practicing mindfulness, and engaging with community narratives that celebrate our shared humanity. Together, we can forge a path toward lasting well-being and enriched relationships.

name: Rebecca Kinman (SHE/THEY)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I earned my Bachelor’s degree in Women, Gender, and Sexuality Studies from Portland State University. I went on to complete a Master’s in Clinical Social Work at the same institution. During my studies, I explored topics like racial equity, housing for LGBTQIAA+ seniors, trauma-informed care, feminist therapy, solution-focused therapy, migration, anti-oppressive models of care. Beyond my academic work, I have post-graduate, on-the-job training in the assessment and treatment of ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder), Bipolar I and II, and Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) among others. I take an eclectic and creative approach to therapy. My work draws from several therapeutic modalities, including Dialectical Behavioral Therapy (DBT), psychodynamic therapy, and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT). I have supported clients dealing with conditions such as depression, anxiety, trauma, ADHD, Autism, gender dysphoria, and others.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I understand that wellness is a privilege. Therefore, we I am committed to challenging and confronting the effects of capitalism racism, ableism, colonialism, misogyny, homophobia, transphobia, and xenophopbia (among others). I especially acknowledge the negative impact of late-stage capitalism on mental health and I do my best to empower clients as the ultimate experts in their own health and well-being. I also believe that the real healing takes place outside of the therapy room. That's why I am passionate about offering an alternative to what is traditionally known as "talk therapy." With consent as the foundation for every session, I work collaboratively with clients to identify personal goals and develop practical strategies that help them carry their new skills into everyday life.

name: Muriel McLachlan (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I am a psychotherapist who holds a Master's Degree in Risk & Prevention (Mental Health Counseling) from Harvard University. I have worked with young children and families in hospital, clinic, school, and community mental health settings. My theoretical orientation is primarily psychodynamic and attachment-based, though I also integrate mindfulness strategies into my work. I have specialized training in nondirective play therapy, collaborative problem solving, internal family systems therapy, and psychodynamic psychotherapy with preadolescent children and adults. I specialize in working with families of young children on a range of issues including anxiety, depression, adjustment difficulties, emotional dysregulation, and parenting challenges.

name: Day Hancock Murphy (THEY/THEM)
approaches:
My Background and Approach
I am a proud trauma survivor and CSA survivor. Living with the effects of trauma on a daily basis is hard, and it can feel like you're the only person in the world who knows what you're going through. Know that you're not alone, and that I'm here to walk alongside you and help you to build a life that's worth cultivating. My approach to therapy is rooted in empathy, authenticity, and a deep commitment to creating a safe, non-judgmental space. I strongly believe in the power of the therapeutic relationship and work diligently to build trust and rapport with each of my clients. I want you to feel seen, heard, and accepted exactly as you are. You're allowed to curse, cry, yell, and do what you need in the space we create to truly embody and express yourself.
My Approach to Helping
As a system therapist, you can expect that I will ask questions about what you are doing outside of yourself to create a full life as we explore what is currently holding you back. What does your support network look like, how do you create joy, do you have hobbies, if spirituality of any kind is a factor in your life, are you doing enough? How can we make more opportunities to connect and build community? How does your privilege, lack of privilege, existence in a white-dominated society impact you? What in the larger world could be impacting your ability to thrive and build? My current modalities that I use first and foremost are IFS and EMDR as well as Narrative and somatic therapies. As many of my clients experience trauma I find that these are models that are particular useful for healing and that connecting with the body through exercises, yoga, taking walks, guided visualizations, can be particularly helpful and have been a large part of my personal journey.

name: Veronica Carmenate
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I have a Ph.D. in Clinical Psychology and several years of experience in outpatient clinics and private practice settings. I have had the privilege of working with individuals of various backgrounds seeking support for a variety of challenges. I practice from a psychodynamic and ACT framework aiming to identify and explore both behavior and thought patterns. I believe in the power of awareness and in the ability to change one’s behavior and life trajectory.
More About the People I Treat
Together we will establish a trusting space to address experienced challenges. I offer a sliding scale fee and can provide a superbill for insurance reimbursement. Please contact me to discuss these options.

name: Jeremy Manalis (HE/HIM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
We will explore any relevant past trauma & family of origin contributions to each of your part of the negative interactions. I will teach you new skills so you will have increased positive interactions. Our therapy will ask you to change how you relate to yourself just as much as how you relate with your partner. I use Relational Life Therapy as my primary approach.
Why I am a Good Fit for You
I also offer consulting services for family-owned businesses and coaching leaders in organizations. I roamed the Sierra Nevada mountains as an Outward Bound Instructor. I have past training and experience in the healing arts as a yoga teacher and Craniosacral Therapist. I am passionate about men’s work: I have facilitated men's process groups and I have been in my own peer-led men's group for 20 years. My undergraduate degree is in Philosophy and Religion from UC Santa Barbara. I hold a Masters degree in Counseling Psychology from Lewis & Clark College. These days when I’m not working, you can usually find me hanging out with my wife and two daughters, making bad dad jokes, going for camping adventures. I enjoy taking acting & improv classes, making electronic music, cooking up a storm, walking my pups, and mountain biking. My #1 self-care is taking a hot sauna followed by a wickedly long cold plunge!

name: Richard Marshall
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Life can sometimes hand us more than we can easily handle. At these times, I help clients navigate life’s trials and transitions. I believe that everyone has abundant natural strengths and capacities to draw from to achieve goals and move closer to their desired life. I collaborate with you to harness and amplify your own strengths in the service of achieving your therapeutic goals, and achieve personal and relational well-being. I provide a warm, inviting, and respectful space for you to explore and express your thoughts and feelings. I view people holistically, considering various life contexts, including biological, psychological, family, social, cultural, spiritual, and also including race, socio-economic class, gender, sexual orientation, and ability. I endeavor to help clients build a sense of interpersonal connection in their lives, by finding their voice, and also by building accountability.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I bring a systemic, culturally-sensitive, relational, and trauma-informed perspective to my work with you. Additionally, I am a lifelong musician and am always open to exploring ways that music or other creative expression can be a positive and supportive part of your life.

name: Katrina Thatcher (SHE/HER)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
I believe you are the expert in your own life. I also believe that life is full of experiences that shape the way we perceive ourselves and relate to others, sometimes creating patterns difficult to untangle on our own. As a counselor, my primary focus is to support you in untangling these social-emotional-behavioral knots that limit your ability to fully embrace and accept your beautiful, unique self. I approach the counseling process as a means for you to better understand yourself and your relationships through the collaborative relationship that exists between client and counselor.

name: Gillian Sleeman
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) and a the more specialized approach of Exposure and Response Prevention have been shown repeatedly to be the most effective treatment interventions for anxiety and related disorders, including OCD. While this is the primary therapeutic orientation used in my practice, I also believe that the relationship between therapist and client is very important in determining therapeutic outcomes. I consider my approach to be open and collaborative and we will work together to identify both long term and shorter term (week to week) goals to help you track your progress and feel that you are making important and lasting changes to your mental health.
My Personal Beliefs and Interests
I graduated from Lewis and Clark College with a degree in Marriage, Couple and Family Therapy. My experience prior to private practice included work in outpatient and residential mental health settings, working with children and adults on issues ranging from addiction to depression to childhood behavioral challenges. I have spent the last 6 years obtaining specialized training in the areas of anxiety and OCD and the use of CBT and ERP as treatment modalities, including completing certification at the Behavioral Therapy Training Institute. I am passionate about this specialty and the power of treatment and consider it a privilege to work along side my clients as they learn about these tools and use them to improve their quality of life.

name: Rhyver Rudick (THEY/THEM, HE/HIM)
approaches:
My Approach to Helping
As a therapist, I greatly honor and celebrate the uniqueness of our own life stories and personalities. The interventions I offer are carefully tailored to your concerns, needs, and personal style. I integrate empirically validated approaches with a mix of traditional therapy and metaphysical healing components. I provide a therapeutic presence that is grounding, nurturing, and sincere. Together, we will combine your own self-knowledge and life expertise with my clinical expertise.
